facade_colour: dark brown-red brick with cream/light grey stone decorative trim and tracery

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has cultural heritage value for its direct association with the Jewish community in Kensington Market and the development of the District as a self contained neighbourhood. Constructed for the oldest Jewish congregation in the District, it was designed by Kaplan & Sprachman, a prolific architectural firm best known for designing movie theatres, as well as synagogues and buildings for the Jewish communities. With its intact built form, building features, and continued use as a place of worship, the property yields information that contributes to the understanding of the important role Toronto’s Jewish community played in the evolution of Kensington Market. Constructed in the Byzantine Revival style, the property has retained many of its architectural details including brick cladding with decorative stonework, twin towers with double-height round arched windows, large entrance stairway, and double entrance with round arched transoms beneath a large round arched window. Completed in 1930, the property is one of only two remaining synagogues in the District.
hcd_data.heritage_register: Part IV Designated (Individual) + Part V HCD

photo_observations.facade_colour: dark brown-red brick with cream/light grey stone decorative trim and tracery
photo_observations.condition_notes: heritage building showing age, one mid-level opening possibly boarded with orange/terracotta panel
photo_observations.window_notes: twin arched pairs at each level; large rose window (circular with quatrefoil/trefoil tracery) centered at upper level; two flanking towers with double-height round arched windows
photo_observations.door_notes: double entrance below, not fully visible in night photo
photo_observations.decorative_elements: stone string courses, decorative stone trim, central gable with decorative stonework, arched window group below rose window
photo_observations.fire_escape_notes: black metal fire escape on right side
photo_observations.notes: Byzantine Revival synagogue (Anshei Minsk). EXCELLENT night photo detail. Part IV Designated heritage building - extremely significant. Large rose window with tracery is key feature. Twin towers with paired round arched windows flank central gable. Orange/terracotta panel visible in center opening at mid-level (possibly painted board-up).

facade_description: 10 St Andrew St presents an institutional facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The principal elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its institutional typology. The principal elevation is faced primarily in brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The elevation is ordered in broad vertical bays, with a 2, 4 opening rhythm from lower to upper levels, using arched, and 2 principal entrances. HCD note: The property has cultural heritage value for its direct association with the Jewish community in Kensington Market and the development of the District as a self contained neighbourhood. Constructed for the oldest Jewish congregation in the District, it was designed by Kaplan & Sprachman, a prolific architectural firm best known for designing movie theatres, as well as synagogues and buildings for the Jewish communities. With its intact built form, building features, and continued use as a place of worship, the property yields information that contributes to the understanding of the important role Toronto’s Jewish community played in the evolution of Kensington Market. Constructed in the Byzantine Revival style, the property has retained many of its architectural details including brick cladding with decorative stonework, twin towers with double-height round arched windows, large entrance stairway, and double entrance with round arched transoms beneath a large round arched window. Completed in 1930, the property is one of only two remaining synagogues in the District.
